subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 2.4 Equations of motion with constant acceleration
questions.question: A car accelerates uniformly from rest to \(20\,\mathrm{m/s}\) in \(5.0\,\mathrm{s}\). The acceleration of the car and the distance traveled are respectively
questions.explanation: \(a = (20 - 0)/5.0 = 4.0\,\mathrm{m/s^2}\); \(s = \frac{1}{2}(4.0)(5.0)^2 = 0.5 \times 4.0 \times 25 = 50\,\mathrm{m}\). Option A uses 2 m/s², C has wrong distance, D has both wrong.

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.4 Equations of motion with constant acceleration

explanations_tiered.definition: Uniform acceleration means constant rate of change of velocity.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: From rest, acceleration = final velocity / time. Distance = (1/2) × acceleration × time².
explanations_tiered.beginner: Car goes from 0 to 20 m/s in 5 s → gains 4 m/s each second (acceleration 4 m/s²). Distance = average speed (10 m/s) × 5 s = 50 m.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Using \(v = v_0 + at\) → \(a = 4\). Then \(s = v_0 t + \frac{1}{2}at^2 = 0 + 0.5 \times 4 \times 25 = 50\) m.
reasoning_steps: Calculate acceleration from velocity change and time.
Use distance formula for constant acceleration from rest.
misconceptions: Using average speed (10 m/s) × time = 50 m works, but must use correct acceleration formula first.

glossary_entry.definition: Constant rate of change of velocity.

flashcard.front: Car from rest to 20 m/s in 5.0 s. Acceleration and distance?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 0 to 20 m/s in 5.0 s: acceleration and distance?

questions.glossary_definition: Constant rate of change of velocity.
questions.teacher_note: Check both parts: acceleration and distance.
questions.learning_objective_tag: Use appropriate equations of motion to solve motion-related problems
questions.workbook_section: 2.4 Equations of motion with constant acceleration

section: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
question: The slope of a velocity-time graph for an object with uniform acceleration is
hint: Slope of v-t graph = acceleration. Uniform acceleration means constant slope.
explanation: For uniform (constant) acceleration, the velocity changes at a constant rate. On a v-t graph, this appears as a straight line with constant slope. A zero slope would mean zero acceleration (constant velocity). Increasing or decreasing slopes would indicate changing acceleration.

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
source_trace.content_anchor: For uniformly accelerated motion, the velocity-time graph is a straight line with constant slope

explanations_tiered.definition: The slope of a velocity-time graph equals acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Uniform acceleration means constant slope (straight line) on a v-t graph. The slope may be positive, negative, or zero.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you're speeding up steadily (constant acceleration), your v-t graph is a straight, slanted line — the slope stays the same all the way.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since \(a = \Delta v/\Delta t\) is constant, \(v\) vs \(t\) is linear: \(v = v_0 + at\). The slope is \(a\), constant.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For constant \(a\), \(dv/dt = a\), so the slope is constant. Non-constant acceleration would produce a curved v-t graph.
reasoning_steps: Recall that slope of v-t graph = acceleration.
Uniform acceleration means acceleration is constant.
Therefore slope is constant (and non-zero unless acceleration is zero).
misconceptions: Thinking a zero slope is the only constant slope (zero acceleration).
Believing constant acceleration means the line must be horizontal.

glossary_entry.definition: Constant acceleration; velocity changes by equal amounts in equal time intervals.

flashcard.front: What is the slope of a v-t graph for uniform acceleration?
flashcard.back: Constant (and non-zero unless acceleration is zero).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the slope of a v-t graph for uniform acceleration?

glossary_definition: Constant acceleration; velocity changes by equal amounts in equal time intervals.
teacher_note: Contrast with non-uniform acceleration where slope changes.
learning_objective_tag: Draw graphs of position-time, velocity-time and acceleration-time graph
workbook_section: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
solution_manual_note: Answer B. Uniform acceleration = constant slope (straight line) on v-t graph.

subject: Physics
question: A runner completes one lap of a 400 m track in 50 s. The magnitude of the average velocity for the lap is
hint: Average velocity depends on displacement, not distance.
explanation: After completing one full lap, the runner returns to the starting point, so displacement = 0 m. Average velocity = displacement/time = 0/50 = 0 m/s. Average speed would be 400/50 = 8 m/s, which is option C.

explanations_tiered.definition: Average velocity is displacement divided by time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a closed loop returning to start, displacement is zero, so average velocity is zero regardless of distance or time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you run around a track and end exactly where you started, your overall change in position is zero, so your average velocity is zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Displacement = 0 m (final position = initial position). Therefore \(v_{av} = 0/50 = 0\) m/s. Average speed (distance/time) = 8 m/s, but that's different.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Average velocity is a vector; for a closed path, the net displacement vector is zero, so the vector average velocity is zero.
worked_solution: Step 1: Identify initial and final positions (same).
reasoning_steps: Recognize that one lap returns to start → displacement = 0.
misconceptions: Using distance (400 m) instead of displacement → 8 m/s (average speed).
Thinking average velocity cannot be zero if you moved.

flashcard.front: One lap of 400 m in 50 s. Average velocity?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 400 m lap in 50 s. Average velocity?

teacher_note: Classic trap: students give average speed instead.
learning_objective_tag: Define instantaneous and average velocity of a body in motion
solution_manual_note: Answer A. Displacement = 0, so average velocity = 0.

subject: Physics
question: A train moves at \(30\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east. A passenger walks at \(2\,\mathrm{m/s}\) west relative to the train. The velocity of the passenger relative to the ground is
explanation: Let east be positive. \(v_{TG} = +30\,\mathrm{m/s}\) (train relative to ground). Passenger relative to train \(v_{PT} = -2\,\mathrm{m/s}\) (west). \(v_{PG} = v_{PT} + v_{TG} = -2 + 30 = 28\,\mathrm{m/s}\) east. Option C adds incorrectly (32), B and D have wrong direction.

source_trace.content_anchor: relative velocity = sum/difference depending on direction

explanations_tiered.definition: The velocity of the passenger relative to ground is the vector sum of the passenger's velocity relative to train and the train's velocity relative to ground.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the train moves east at 30 m/s and you walk west at 2 m/s relative to the train, your ground speed is 28 m/s east (the train's speed minus your walking speed).
misconceptions: Subtracting in wrong order (30 - 2 = 28 is correct magnitude but must ensure direction correct).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Opposite directions: subtract magnitudes; direction of larger.

glossary_entry.definition: Velocity as measured from another moving reference frame.

flashcard.front: Train 30 m/s east, passenger walks 2 m/s west relative to train. Passenger's ground velocity?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Train 30 m/s east, passenger walks 2 m/s west relative to train. Ground velocity?

glossary_definition: Velocity as measured from another moving reference frame.
teacher_note: Use sign convention to avoid errors.

subject: Physics
question: The instantaneous acceleration of an object is the slope of the tangent line on which type of graph at a specific instant?
hint: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time.
explanation: Instantaneous acceleration is the derivative of velocity with respect to time. On a velocity-time graph, the slope of the tangent at any point gives the instantaneous acceleration. Position-time graph slope gives velocity, and acceleration-time graph slope gives jerk.

source_trace.content_anchor: slope of velocity-time graph gives acceleration

explanations_tiered.definition: Instantaneous acceleration is the slope of the tangent on a velocity-time graph.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Because \(a = dv/dt\), the derivative of velocity with respect to time is the slope of the v-t curve.
explanations_tiered.beginner: On a velocity-time graph, how steep the line is at one point tells you the acceleration right then. Steeper slope = larger acceleration.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For any v-t graph, \(a = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \Delta v/\Delta t\) = slope of tangent. This is instantaneous acceleration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The derivative \(dv/dt\) is the instantaneous slope of the v(t) function. Position-time gives velocity; velocity-time gives acceleration.
reasoning_steps: On a v-t graph, \(dv/dt\) is the slope.
Thus, slope of tangent on v-t graph = instantaneous acceleration.
misconceptions: Confusing with position-time graph (slope gives velocity).
Thinking acceleration-time graph slope gives acceleration (it gives jerk).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Instantaneous acceleration = slope of v-t graph.

glossary_entry.definition: The derivative of velocity with respect to time; slope of v-t graph.

flashcard.front: How is instantaneous acceleration obtained from a velocity-time graph?
flashcard.back: Slope of the tangent line at that instant.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which graph's tangent slope gives instantaneous acceleration?

glossary_definition: The derivative of velocity with respect to time; slope of v-t graph.
teacher_note: Contrast with position-time (slope = velocity) and acceleration-time (slope = jerk).
learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between instantaneous acceleration and average acceleration using graphical method
solution_manual_note: Answer B. Slope of velocity-time graph = acceleration.

subject: Physics
question: A person walks 10 m north, then 8 m south, then 6 m north. The magnitude of the displacement is
hint: Displacement is net change: north positive, south negative.
explanation: Let north be positive. Net displacement = 10 - 8 + 6 = 8 m north. Magnitude = 8 m. Total distance = 10 + 8 + 6 = 24 m (option D), which is the distance, not displacement.

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement is the net change in position, considering direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Assign signs to directions (north +, south -), then sum. Magnitude is absolute value.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you go north 10 steps, back south 8 steps, then north 6 steps, you end up 8 steps north of where you started — displacement 8 m north.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Net displacement = 10 (north) − 8 (south) + 6 (north) = 8 m north. Magnitude = 8 m.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Displacement is the vector sum: \(\vec{s} = 10\hat{j} - 8\hat{j} + 6\hat{j} = 8\hat{j}\) m.
misconceptions: Adding absolute values (10+8+6=24) gives distance, not displacement.

glossary_entry.definition: Net change in position, a vector.

flashcard.front: 10 m N, 8 m S, 6 m N. Displacement magnitude?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 10 m N, 8 m S, 6 m N. Displacement magnitude?

glossary_definition: Net change in position, a vector.
teacher_note: Emphasize that distance is 24 m, displacement is 8 m.
learning_objective_tag: Describe the difference between distance and displacement

subject: Physics
section: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
question: For an object moving with constant velocity, the acceleration-time graph is a

options.B: Horizontal line at a positive value
options.C: Horizontal line at a negative value

explanation: If velocity is constant, acceleration = 0. On an acceleration-time graph, constant zero acceleration appears as a horizontal line at a = 0. Positive or negative horizontal lines would indicate constant non-zero acceleration, which would change velocity.

source_trace.section_anchor: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
source_trace.content_anchor: For a uniformly accelerated motion, acceleration is constant with time. Hence, acceleration time-graph will be a straight line parallel to the time axis

explanations_tiered.definition: An acceleration-time graph plots acceleration vs. time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Constant velocity → zero acceleration → horizontal line at a = 0.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you're going at a steady speed (constant velocity), your acceleration is zero. On an a-t graph, that's a flat line at zero.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Constant velocity means \(a = 0\) at all times. The graph is the line \(a(t) = 0\), a horizontal line on the time axis.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For constant velocity, \(dv/dt = 0\). Therefore \(a(t) = 0\), which is a horizontal line at zero on an a-t graph.
reasoning_steps: Recall that constant velocity means no change in velocity.
Acceleration = rate of change of velocity = 0.
On a-t graph, constant zero is a horizontal line at 0.
misconceptions: Thinking constant velocity means constant acceleration (non-zero).
Believing a horizontal line must be positive or negative.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Constant velocity → a = 0 → horizontal line at zero on a-t.

glossary_entry.definition: Motion with no change in speed or direction; acceleration = 0.

flashcard.front: Constant velocity: what does the acceleration-time graph look like?

glossary_definition: Motion with no change in speed or direction; acceleration = 0.
teacher_note: Contrast with constant acceleration (horizontal line but not at zero).
learning_objective_tag: Draw graphs of position-time, velocity-time and acceleration-time graph
workbook_section: 2.5 Graphical representation of uniformly accelerated motion
solution_manual_note: Answer A. Constant velocity → a = 0 → horizontal line at zero.

subject: Physics
question: The instantaneous velocity of an object is the limit of the average velocity as
hint: Instantaneous quantities are defined as limits as time interval → 0.
explanation: Instantaneous velocity is defined as \(v = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}\). This is the derivative of position with respect to time. The time interval must go to zero; displacement also goes to zero in the limit, but the key is the time interval.

explanations_tiered.definition: Instantaneous velocity is the limit of average velocity as the time interval approaches zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This limit gives the derivative \(ds/dt\), the exact velocity at a moment.
explanations_tiered.beginner: To find your exact speed at one instant, you look at a super short time interval — as short as possible (approaching zero).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(v = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}\). The time interval \(\Delta t\) must approach zero, not displacement or position.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is the definition of the derivative: \(v(t) = \frac{ds}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{s(t+\Delta t) - s(t)}{\Delta t}\).
reasoning_steps: Recall the definition of instantaneous velocity.
It involves taking the limit as the time interval goes to zero.
misconceptions: Thinking \(\Delta s \to 0\) defines it (that would give 0/0 ambiguity).
Confusing with average velocity over finite interval.

glossary_entry.definition: The limit of average velocity as time interval approaches zero; derivative of position with respect to time.

flashcard.front: How is instantaneous velocity defined as a limit?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Limit of average velocity as ____ approaches zero gives instantaneous velocity?

glossary_definition: The limit of average velocity as time interval approaches zero; derivative of position with respect to time.
learning_objective_tag: Define instantaneous and average velocity of a body in motion

subject: Physics
question: A plane flies with a velocity of 200 km/h north relative to the air. There is a wind of 50 km/h south. The velocity of the plane relative to the ground is
explanation: North positive. Plane relative to air = +200 km/h, air relative to ground (wind) = -50 km/h. Plane relative to ground = 200 + (-50) = 150 km/h north. Option C adds magnitudes (250 north) which would be with tailwind (south wind helping north).

explanations_tiered.definition: Relative velocity of plane to ground = velocity of plane relative to air + velocity of air relative to ground.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Headwind (opposing) reduces ground speed; tailwind increases it. Here wind is opposite to plane's heading, so ground speed = 200 - 50 = 150 km/h north.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you fly north at 200 km/h but the wind blows south at 50 km/h, your ground speed is only 150 km/h north — the wind pushes you back.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Using Galilean transformation: \(\vec{v}_{PG} = \vec{v}_{PA} - \vec{v}_{GA}\), but careful with signs. Standard formula: \(v_{PG} = v_{PA} + v_{AG}\).
misconceptions: Adding magnitudes (200+50=250) gives tailwind case, not headwind.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Headwind: ground speed = airspeed − wind speed.

glossary_entry.definition: Velocity as measured from another moving reference frame.

flashcard.front: Plane 200 km/h north, wind 50 km/h south. Ground velocity?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 200 km/h north, wind 50 km/h south. Ground speed?

glossary_definition: Velocity as measured from another moving reference frame.
teacher_note: Contrast with tailwind where speeds add.
